7209	\begin{proof}
7210	  \uses{def:cech_augmented_complex, lem:section_cech_homology_exact,
7211	    lem:affine_cech_vanishing_tilde_subcover, def:cohomology_sheaf_is_sheafify_homology,

7213	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
7214	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof).}
7215	  We prove exactness of the augmented {\v C}ech complex of
7216	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_augmented_complex} in \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\) by showing that each homology
7217	  object \(\mathcal{H}^p\) (\(p \geq 1\), together with the augmentation node) is the zero object. The
7218	  argument works entirely with sections and sheafification, never with stalks.
7219	
7220	  \emph{Step 1: reflect through the forgetful functor to abelian sheaves.} The forgetful functor from
7221	  sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to sheaves of abelian groups is faithful and additive, hence
7222	  preserves zero morphisms; a faithful additive functor reflects the property of an object being zero.
7223	  It therefore suffices to prove that the image of each homology object \(\mathcal{H}^p\) under this
7224	  forgetful functor is zero in the category of abelian sheaves.
7225	
7226	  \emph{Step 2: the homology sheaf is the sheafification of the presheaf homology.} By the
7227	  sheafification-commutes-with-homology engine of
7228	  Lemma~\ref{def:cohomology_sheaf_is_sheafify_homology}, the degree-\(p\) homology of the complex of
7229	  abelian sheaves is the sheafification of the degree-\(p\) homology of the underlying complex of
7230	  \emph{presheaves}. The presheaf homology evaluated at an open \(V\) is the homology of the section
7231	  complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})(V)\) --- the {\v C}ech cochain complex
7232	  of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the cover restricted to \(V\) --- so the presheaf in question is
7233	  \(V \mapsto \check{H}^p(V, \mathcal{F})\).
7234	
7235	  \emph{Step 3: the presheaf homology vanishes locally on the affine basis.} This presheaf is not zero
7236	  as a presheaf, but it vanishes on a basis. Over any basic affine \(D(g)\) small enough to sit inside
7237	  a single cover element \(U_i\), the affine tilde isomorphism
7238	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_isIso_fromTildeGamma}) identifies \(\mathcal{F}|_{D(g)}\) with \(\widetilde{M}\),
7239	  and the section {\v C}ech complex of \(\widetilde{M}\) over the induced standard cover is exact in
7240	  positive degrees: this is precisely Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_cech_vanishing_tilde_subcover} (whose
7241	  section-level exactness packaging is Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}). Since the basic
7242	  affines contained in the cover elements form a basis cofinal among all opens, the canonical map
7243	  \(0 \to (\text{presheaf homology})\) is a local isomorphism --- it is locally bijective. A map of
7244	  sheaves is an isomorphism if and only if it is locally bijective, and sheafification carries a locally
7245	  bijective map to an isomorphism; hence the sheafification of the presheaf homology is the zero sheaf.
7246	  Combined with Step 2 this gives \(\mathcal{H}^p = 0\) for all \(p \geq 1\).
7247	
7248	  \emph{Step 4: the augmentation node.} Separately, the augmentation \(\varepsilon : \mathcal{F} \to
7249	  \mathcal{C}^0\) is the kernel inclusion: the degree-\(0\) homology of the augmented complex vanishes
7250	  because over each basic affine the augmented section complex is exact at degree \(0\) (the same
7251	  spanning-family argument that gives exactness in positive degrees produces exactness at the
7252	  augmentation node), and sheafifying this local exactness as in Step 3 makes the augmentation node
7253	  homology zero. Together with Steps 1--3, every homology object of the augmented complex is zero, so
7254	  the complex is exact and the {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} is a resolution of
7255	  \(\mathcal{F}\) in \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\).
7256	
7257	  \emph{Remark.} An alternative proof uses the stalk-at-prime criterion directly: the complex is
7258	  exact if and only if it is exact after localizing at each prime \(\mathfrak{p}\), and for each
7259	  \(\mathfrak{p}\) one fixes an index \(i_{\text{fix}}\) with \(f_{i_{\text{fix}}} \notin \mathfrak{p}\)
7260	  and defines a contracting homotopy of the localized complex by
7261	  \(h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_{\text{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}\), as in the Stacks source proof above.
7262	  This approach is equivalent to the sections-and-sheafification argument of Steps 1--4.
7263	\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[Sheafification kills a presheaf that is locally bijective to a locally-zero one]
  \label{lem:sheafify_kills_locally_zero}
  \lean{CategoryTheory.GrothendieckTopology.isZero_presheafToSheaf_obj_of_W,
    CategoryTheory.GrothendieckTopology.isZero_presheafToSheaf_obj_of_W_isZero,
    CategoryTheory.GrothendieckTopology.isZero_presheafToSheaf_obj_of_isLocallyBijective}
  \textit{Project-bespoke site-theory lemma (pure Mathlib site theory).}
  Let \(J\) be a Grothendieck topology on a site \(\mathcal{C}\) and let \(J.W\) denote the
  class of \emph{locally bijective} maps of presheaves --- the local-isomorphism class for
  \(J\), i.e.\ the maps inverted by sheafification. Then:
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item Sheafification \(\operatorname{presheafToSheaf}\) carries every map in \(J.W\) to an
      isomorphism; consequently it sends a presheaf that is locally bijective to a presheaf
      with zero sheafification to a presheaf with zero sheafification. Precisely, if
      \(\varphi : P \to Q\) lies in \(J.W\) and the sheafification of \(Q\) is the zero
      sheaf, then the sheafification of \(P\) is the zero sheaf.
    \item In particular, if \(P\) admits a locally bijective map to the zero presheaf --- for
      instance if \(P\) is locally zero, i.e.\ vanishes on a basis of the topology --- then
      the sheafification of \(P\) is the zero sheaf.
    \item For an abelian-valued presheaf the locally-bijective hypothesis is the conjunction
      of local injectivity and local surjectivity of the comparison map; a map that is both
      locally injective and locally surjective lies in \(J.W\).
  \end{enumerate}
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  Sheafification \(\operatorname{presheafToSheaf} : \widehat{\mathcal{C}} \to
  \operatorname{Sh}(J)\) inverts exactly the maps of the class \(J.W\): this is the
  characterization \(\varphi \in J.W \iff \operatorname{presheafToSheaf}(\varphi)\text{ is an
  isomorphism}\) (the Mathlib fact \texttt{GrothendieckTopology.W\_iff}). Hence for
  \(\varphi : P \to Q\) in \(J.W\) the induced map \(\operatorname{sheafify}(P) \to
  \operatorname{sheafify}(Q)\) is an isomorphism, and being a zero object transports along an
  isomorphism: if \(\operatorname{sheafify}(Q)\) is zero then so is
  \(\operatorname{sheafify}(P)\). This proves (1); part (2) is the special case
  \(Q = 0\) (the zero presheaf, whose sheafification is the zero sheaf), and a presheaf that
  vanishes on a basis admits such a locally bijective map to \(0\). For (3), in the abelian
  setting sheafification is additive (\texttt{presheafToSheaf\_additive}) and a map of
  abelian presheaves that is simultaneously locally injective and locally surjective is a
  local isomorphism, i.e.\ lies in \(J.W\)
  (\texttt{GrothendieckTopology.W\_of\_isLocallyBijective}); applying (1) then collapses its
  sheafification.
\end{proof}
